title: The Group Chat
introduction: Read this group chat conversation. Morgan is watching what's happening to their classmate Cian. Tap any message you think is bullying or a warning sign, then press Submit.

script.text: JAKE: did everyone see what Cian wore today 😭😭 someone tell him the 90s called
script.explanation: Mocking someone's appearance in a group chat — even framed as a joke — is cyberbullying. The fact that multiple people can see and respond amplifies the harm significantly compared to a one-to-one comment.

script.text: SOPHIE: LMAO literally. Hey @morgan back me up, you think it's funny too right?
script.explanation: Calling out a bystander by name to validate the bullying is a tactic to expand the pile-on and normalise it. It also puts Morgan in an uncomfortable position — agree and participate, or risk becoming a target themselves.

script.text: JAKE: someone screenshotted his story where he's crying about some game lmaooo I'm gonna post it in the year group chat
script.explanation: Sharing someone's private post or image without their consent — especially to mock them — is a serious form of cyberbullying. Depending on the content and age of the person, it may also be illegal.

script.text: I don't think you should post that
JAKE: why are you defending him? are you his boyfriend or something 😂 don't be weird Morgan
script.explanation: When Morgan tried to stop the behaviour, they immediately became a secondary target. This is how bystanders are kept silent — anyone who objects risks the same treatment. It's a deliberate silencing tactic.

script.text: SOPHIE: Cian you should just leave school tbh. nobody actually likes you here. it would be better for everyone 🙂
script.flag_label: Telling someone to leave / severe exclusion
script.explanation: Telling someone nobody likes them and that they should leave is severe bullying with real potential to cause serious mental harm. Messages like this — especially when Cian can see the entire group agreeing — should be reported immediately to a trusted adult or school.

script.text: I'm going to screenshot this and show a teacher

learning_objectives: Cyberbullying in group chats is still bullying — the audience makes it worse, not better
Being pressured to join in doesn't make participation acceptable
Sharing someone's private content without consent is a serious harm
Pushing back on bullying is the right thing — even if you become a target
Screenshots and reporting to a trusted adult is always the right move
debrief:
This group chat shows how cyberbullying escalates — from a joke, to mockery, to private content being shared, to severe exclusion — all within a few messages.

**What made this worse:** The group format means Cian saw multiple people laughing. Being excluded by a group is significantly more harmful than one person's comment. The pile-on effect is real.

**Morgan's instinct was right** — pushing back and threatening to screenshot was the correct response. It took courage and it worked.

**If you're Cian:** What's happening to you is not your fault. Screenshot everything, block the worst accounts, and tell an adult — a parent, teacher, or school counsellor. You don't have to deal with this alone.

**If you're Morgan:** Reporting to a teacher, parent, or the platform is the most effective action. Screenshots preserve the evidence.
